items.title: Executive order reclassifies about 8,000 federal positions into Schedule Policy/Career
items.summary: On June 3, President Trump signed an executive order moving roughly 8,000 senior policy-influencing federal positions into a new Schedule Policy/Career classification, removing certain civil-service job protections.
items.details: The order creates a new Schedule Policy/Career category covering roughly 8,000 senior federal roles that help shape policy. Employees in these positions lose certain civil-service protections, making them easier to remove. The administration describes it as increasing accountability; opponents say it could weaken civil-service safeguards.

items.title: Executive order on advanced AI innovation and security
items.summary: An executive order published in the Federal Register on June 5 directs federal agencies to support AI innovation and strengthen cybersecurity of government and critical-infrastructure systems.
items.details: The executive order directs federal agencies to promote development of advanced artificial intelligence while strengthening cybersecurity for government and critical-infrastructure systems. It was published in the Federal Register on June 5, 2026. It is one of several recent federal actions addressing AI.

items.title: National security memorandum directs faster AI adoption in military and intelligence
items.summary: On June 5, the President signed National Security Presidential Memorandum NSPM-11 directing the national-security enterprise to accelerate adoption of advanced AI systems, rescinding the prior NSM-25.
items.details: The national security memorandum directs defense and intelligence agencies to speed up adoption of advanced AI systems. It replaces the earlier NSM-25. The action is part of a broader effort to integrate artificial intelligence across the national-security enterprise.

items.title: Israel and Iran trade heaviest strikes in months as ceasefire falters
items.summary: Israel and Iran exchanged some of their heaviest strikes in months as a months-old ceasefire faltered, with explosions reported in Tehran, Isfahan, and Tabriz.
items.details: A months-old ceasefire between Israel and Iran came under strain as both sides exchanged heavy strikes. Explosions were reported in the Iranian cities of Tehran, Isfahan, and Tabriz. The exchange was described as among the most intense in months.

items.title: U.S. forces strike Iranian coastal radar sites after drones launched toward Strait of Hormuz
items.summary: U.S. forces struck Iranian coastal radar stations after Tehran launched attack drones toward the Strait of Hormuz, further straining the ceasefire as peace negotiations stalled.
items.details: U.S. forces struck Iranian coastal radar installations after Iran launched drones toward the Strait of Hormuz, a key oil-shipping route. The action added pressure to a fragile ceasefire. Diplomatic talks between the two sides were stalled at the time.

items.title: U.S. and Iran negotiating a 60-day cessation-of-hostilities proposal
items.summary: Reports describe a proposed memorandum for a 60-day pause in hostilities during which the U.S. and Iran would negotiate issues including uranium enrichment and sanctions relief.
items.details: Reporting describes a proposed memorandum under which the U.S. and Iran would pause hostilities for 60 days. During that window the two sides would negotiate issues such as uranium enrichment and the lifting of sanctions. Terms had not been finalized.

items.title: U.S. Treasury continues Iran sanctions during ceasefire talks
items.summary: The Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control continued sanctions pressure during negotiations, designating the Persian Gulf Strait Authority on May 27 as part of its 'Economic Fury' campaign.
items.details: Even during ceasefire negotiations, the U.S. Treasury continued sanctions on Iran. On May 27 its Office of Foreign Assets Control designated the Persian Gulf Strait Authority. Officials described the move as part of an ongoing economic-pressure campaign.

items.title: Senate passes $70B immigration enforcement bill (S.2, Secure America Act)
items.summary: The Senate voted 52-47 early June 5 to pass S.2, a reconciliation bill funding Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol for three years.
items.details: The Senate passed S.2, the Secure America Act, by a 52-47 vote early on June 5. The reconciliation package provides about $70 billion to fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol for three years. Because it is a reconciliation bill, it advanced with a simple majority.

items.title: Supreme Court lets Alabama use contested congressional map for 2026 elections
items.summary: In an emergency order, the Supreme Court cleared the way for Alabama to use a congressional map in the 2026 elections that a lower court had found racially discriminatory.
items.details: The Supreme Court issued an emergency order allowing Alabama to use a congressional map for the 2026 elections that a lower court had found racially discriminatory. The decision affects how district lines are drawn for the midterms. Litigation over the map continues.

items.title: Supreme Court rules FCC forfeiture process does not violate Seventh Amendment
items.summary: In FCC v. AT&T, the Supreme Court ruled 8-1 that the FCC's process for issuing monetary forfeiture orders does not violate the Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial.
items.details: The Supreme Court ruled 8-1 in FCC v. AT&T that the agency's process for issuing monetary forfeiture orders does not violate the Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial. The decision concerns how federal regulators impose financial penalties.

items.title: Federal judge strikes down policy halting asylum decisions for 39 countries
items.summary: A federal judge struck down a policy that had barred immigrants from 39 countries from receiving final decisions on asylum, work permit, green card, and citizenship applications.
items.details: A federal judge struck down a policy that had paused final decisions on asylum, work-permit, green-card, and citizenship applications for people from 39 countries. The ruling allows those applications to be decided. The government may appeal.

items.title: Voters in six states cast ballots in June 2 primaries
items.summary: On June 2, voters in California, Iowa, Montana, New Jersey, New Mexico, and South Dakota voted in primaries setting up November midterm matchups for governor, Senate, and House.
items.details: Voters in California, Iowa, Montana, New Jersey, New Mexico, and South Dakota cast primary ballots on June 2. The results set up November midterm matchups for governor, Senate, and House seats across those states.

items.title: Analysis: remaining options for minority voting-rights challenges after recent rulings
items.summary: A June 5 report examined the legal alternatives available for challenging redistricting on racial grounds following recent Supreme Court decisions on the Voting Rights Act.
items.details: Following recent Supreme Court decisions on the Voting Rights Act, this report examines the legal options that remain for challenging redistricting on racial grounds. It outlines how future cases might proceed and what standards courts may apply.

AZ.title: Arizona budget negotiations with Gov. Hobbs nearly complete, Senate president says
AZ.summary: Senate President Warren Petersen said lawmakers and Gov. Katie Hobbs were about 97% toward a state budget deal, with a June 30 deadline to avoid a shutdown.

AR.title: Arkansas TV Commission votes to keep PBS affiliation with privately raised funds
AR.summary: On June 4 the Arkansas TV Commission voted to accept more than $2.1 million in privately raised funds to pay PBS dues through June 2027, retaining the state's PBS affiliation.

CO.title: Colorado Gov. Polis vetoes three bills, including a 'surveillance pricing' measure
CO.summary: Gov. Jared Polis on June 2 vetoed three bills, including measures on surveillance-based pricing, arbitration, and single-use restaurant utensils.

CT.title: Connecticut Gov. Lamont signs youth online-safety and AI law
CT.summary: On June 2, Gov. Ned Lamont signed Public Act 26-15, setting requirements for social-media platforms used by minors and rules for artificial intelligence and chatbots.

DE.title: Gov. Meyer nominates Vice Chancellor Morgan Zurn to Delaware Supreme Court
DE.summary: On June 5, Gov. Matt Meyer nominated Court of Chancery Vice Chancellor Morgan Zurn to the Delaware Supreme Court, pending state Senate confirmation.

FL.title: Florida Legislature places homestead property-tax exemption amendment on November ballot
FL.summary: During a June special session, the Legislature passed a measure placing a constitutional amendment to expand the homestead property-tax exemption before voters in November 2026.

GA.title: Georgia Republican gubernatorial runoff set for June 16
GA.summary: Georgia's Republican primary runoff for governor between Lt. Gov. Burt Jones and Rick Jackson is scheduled for June 16; the winner faces Democrat Keisha Lance Bottoms in November.

HI.title: Hawaii Gov. Green weighs vetoes from 2026 legislative session
HI.summary: Gov. Josh Green said 268 bills from the 2026 session await action, with his intent-to-veto list due by June 30.

MI.title: Michigan Senate proposes changing how university boards are selected
MI.summary: Senate leadership released a proposed constitutional amendment that would let the governor appoint members of the University of Michigan, Michigan State, and Wayne State governing boards.

MN.title: Minnesota Legislature passes budget in one-day special session
MN.summary: On June 9, Minnesota lawmakers convened a one-day special session and passed the remaining state budget bills, averting a government shutdown and pending layoff notices.

NH.title: New Hampshire Legislature sends three housing bills to Gov. Ayotte
NH.summary: On the final session day, the House and Senate passed HB 1010, HB 1588, and SB 564 addressing housing in commercial zones, parking rules, and dead-end roads.

NM.title: Deb Haaland wins New Mexico Democratic primary for governor
NM.summary: Former Interior Secretary Deb Haaland won the June 2 Democratic gubernatorial primary, defeating Sam Bregman, and will face Republican Gregg Hull in November.

NY.title: New York Legislature passes one-year data-center moratorium
NY.summary: New York's Legislature passed a bill on June 4 imposing a one-year moratorium on permits for large data centers, sending it to Gov. Hochul.

NC.title: North Carolina legislature overrides veto to enact scholarship tax credit
NC.summary: On June 3, the North Carolina Senate voted 30-19 to complete a veto override of House Bill 87, enacting a tax credit for donations to scholarship-granting organizations.

ND.title: North Dakota holds statewide primary election
ND.summary: North Dakota voters cast ballots in the statewide primary election on June 9, with polls closing at 7 p.m. local time.

OH.title: Ohio governor pauses new data-center tax-exemption requests
OH.summary: Gov. Mike DeWine directed the Ohio Tax Credit Authority to stop accepting new data-center sales-tax exemption requests while a legislative committee studies the issue.

OK.title: Oklahoma to hold statewide primary June 16, including open governor's race
OK.summary: Oklahoma holds its statewide primary on June 16, with an open governor's race because incumbent Kevin Stitt is term-limited.

UT.title: Utah Gov. Cox appoints two justices to expanded state Supreme Court
UT.summary: On June 2, Gov. Spencer Cox appointed Jay Jorgensen and Stephen Dent to two new Utah Supreme Court seats created by 2026 legislation, pending Senate confirmation.
UT.source: Office of Governor Spencer J. Cox

disclaimer: These headlines are gathered from reputable news organizations and official sources and presented factually, without partisan commentary. Each item links to its original source so you can read more and verify. OurVoiceVote is non-partisan and does not endorse any party, candidate, or position. Refreshed automatically each day at 9:00 AM Central.
